coredata.dc:title: Highly active carbon dots based nanozyme for enhanced cholesterol detection and antibacterial activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhi
Yang, Qingyuan
Zhou, Peng
Li, Cao
Wang, Xiaoying
Jiang, Bingbing
Wang, Qian
Xu, Ziqiang
coredata.dc:description: Recently, nanozyme have emerged as promising direction for chemical sensing, biomedicine, environmental monitoring. However, the novel nanozyme with better catalytic activity and specificity is urgently required realized great application performance. Herein, Fe doped carbon dots (Cit-Fe-CDs) with peroxidase (POD) like activity are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of ferric ammonium citrate. Due to the strong coordination capacity between Fe(Ⅲ) and citric acid, the Cit-Fe-CDs exhibited excellent POD activity. Namely the catalytic constant (K cat) and catalytic efficiency (K cat /K m) are determined, which are much higher than previous work. Highly effective colorimetric detection of cholesterol in human serum have been realized by the POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs. Under optimal conditions, the linear range is determined to be 10 and 100 μM with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.061 μM. The LOD is two orders of magnitude lower than most of the existing analysis method. Since substantial amounts of free radicals are involved in the catalytic cycle of POD activity, excellent antibacterial activity against Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) should be realized by Cit-Fe-CDs. Notably, about 99.999 % inactivation rate towards MRSA can be realized by 125 μg/mL Cit-Fe-CDs, which showcases promising prospects in addressing bacterial resistance concerns. And we have confirmed that the antibacterials mechanism is related to the generation of hydroxyl radicals by the excellent POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxidase like nanozyme
Colorimetric detection
Antibacterial

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: POD like and OXD-like activities of Cit-Fe-CDs
Antibacterial activity and ROS generation of Cit-Fe-CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, HRTEM image (inset), the size distribution of the Cit-Fe-CDs (inset). (B) XRD spectrum and (C) FT-IR spectrum of Cit-Fe-CDs. XPS spectrum of (D) N1s region and (E) O1s region of Cit-Fe-CDs. (F) UV–vis absorption and FL emission (inset) spectra of Cit-Fe-CDs.

If no special explanation, then pH = 4, [Cit-Fe-CDs] = 25 μg/mL, [TMB], [oPD], [ABTS], [H2O2] = 1 mM. (A) UV−vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems corresponding pictures (inset). (B) Absorption spectra of Cit-Fe-CDs catalyzed by three chromogenic substrates and corresponding pictures (inset). (C) Absorbance of TMB catalyzed by different concentrations of Cit-Fe-CDs. (D) Absorbance of Cit-Fe-CDs catalyzed by different concentrations of TMB. (E) UV absorption spectra of Cit-Fe-CDs reacted with H2O2 and various active oxygen scavengers; (F) EPR spectra of POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs. Cit-Fe-CDs catalyze the absorbance of TMB by (G) peroxidases and (H) oxidases under different pH conditions. (I) Absorbance of Cit-Fe-CDs and HRP catalyzed TMB at different temperatures.

Absorbance at 652 nm and reaction time curves of colorimetric reactions catalyzed by Cit-Fe-CDs with different concentrations of (A) TMB and (D) H2O2. Fitted Michaelis-Menten curves of different concentrations of (B) TMB and (E) H2O2. (C) and (F) corresponding to the Lineweaver-Burk plots of different concentrations of TMB and H2O2 for the steady-state kinetic analysis of Cit-Fe-CDs.

(A) Absorbance of cholesterol reacted with different concentrations of ChOx after adding Cit-Fe-CDs and TMB. (B) Absorbance for mechanistic studies of cholesterol detection. (C) Absorbance of different concentrations of cholesterol. (D) Absorbance of different concentrations of cholesterol at 652 nm and corresponding pictures (concentration increasing with the arrow, inset). (E) Linear fitting of absorbance of cholesterol concentration in the range of 10–100 μM. (F) Absorbance at 652 nm of different interfering substances.

(A) Growth rate of MRSA at different concentrations of Cit-Fe-CDs. (B, D) MRSA colony count at different concentrations of Cit-Fe-CDs. (C) Assay of intracellular reactive oxygen species in MRSA cells with and without added Cit-Fe-CDs.
(A) Live/dead bacterial viability assay and (B) bacterial morphology damage assay of MRSA after different treatments.
Schematic illustration for the fabrication of Cit-Fe-CDs and their application in Cholesterol Detection and MRSA elimination.
Detection of Cholesterol in Human Serum.

head.ce:title: Highly active carbon dots based nanozyme for enhanced cholesterol detection and antibacterial activity

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhi Chen and Qingyuan Yang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, nanozyme have emerged as promising direction for chemical sensing, biomedicine, environmental monitoring. However, the novel nanozyme with better catalytic activity and specificity is urgently required realized great application performance. Herein, Fe doped carbon dots (Cit-Fe-CDs) with peroxidase (POD) like activity are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of ferric ammonium citrate. Due to the strong coordination capacity between Fe(Ⅲ) and citric acid, the Cit-Fe-CDs exhibited excellent POD activity. Namely the catalytic constant (K cat) and catalytic efficiency (K cat /K m) are determined, which are much higher than previous work. Highly effective colorimetric detection of cholesterol in human serum have been realized by the POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs. Under optimal conditions, the linear range is determined to be 10 and 100 μM with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.061 μM. The LOD is two orders of magnitude lower than most of the existing analysis method. Since substantial amounts of free radicals are involved in the catalytic cycle of POD activity, excellent antibacterial activity against Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) should be realized by Cit-Fe-CDs. Notably, about 99.999 % inactivation rate towards MRSA can be realized by 125 μg/mL Cit-Fe-CDs, which showcases promising prospects in addressing bacterial resistance concerns. And we have confirmed that the antibacterials mechanism is related to the generation of hydroxyl radicals by the excellent POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 Schematic illustration for the fabrication of Cit-Fe-CDs and their application in Cholesterol Detection and MRSA elimination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional nanomaterials imitate the catalytic functionality of natural enzymes by nanostructured artificial enzymes [1,2], which can be defined as nanozymes. Compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes exhibit higher catalytic stability, lower manufacturing/storage costs, and facile modification [2], which can be considered as ideal substitute of the nanozymes. According to the catalytic mechanism, the types of natural enzymes can be divided into seven types, including oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, ligases, and translocases [3]. The imitation to the natural enzymes by nanozyme is mainly focused on the oxidoreductases such as peroxidase (POD), oxidase (OXD), catalase (CAT), and superoxide dismutase (SOD), while a small part of them focused on the hydrolase or others [4]. POD-like activity is a class of redox reactions that use H2O2 as the reaction substrate and can imitate the activity of horseradish peroxidases, depending on the types of hydrogen donors [5]. It should be noted that POD like nanozyme is the most important nanozyme, which is usually constituted of transition metal and noble metal [6]. At present, the reported nanozymes with the enzyme-like activity have been widely applied in various biomedical application, such as the biosensing, anti-microorganism therapy, cancer therapy and antioxidation therapy [3]. However, high activity serves as a prerequisite for their practical application, especially the diagnostics or anti-microorganism therapy [7], and metal ions doping represents an effective strategy to enhance their activity [8]. This enhancement of catalytic activity significantly affects performance of biological detection and antibacterial applications.

Cholesterol is an indispensable substance in both human and animal bodies. It plays a vital role as a major component of animal cell membranes, contributing to the stability and fluidity of cell membrane structure [9,10]. However, elevated cholesterol levels in human serum would contribute to numerous severe health conditions, including hypertension, coronary heart disease, heart attacks, and cerebral thrombosis. In addition, extremely low cholesterol levels can result in anemia, sepsis, and malnutrition-related ailments [11]. Therefore, monitoring and surveillance of cholesterol levels in human serum have become pivotal in preventing the occurrence of diseases stemming from excessively high cholesterol levels. Traditional cholesterol detection primarily utilizes several techniques, including electrochemistry [12], liquid chromatography [13], fluorescence analysis [14], and colorimetry [15]. However, some of these methods suffer from issues, such as complex operation, expensive equipment requirements, and low sensitivity [16,17]. Colorimetry, as a simple, rapid, cost-effective method that allows results to be observed with the naked eye, has garnered extensive research attention. Nevertheless, colorimetry also faces challenges related to stability and susceptibility to interference from other substances in the coloration reaction. The key to overcome these issues is relied on the POD like nanozyme with ultra high catalytic activity. Currently, various nanozymes, such as Fe3O4 nanoparticles [18], graphene nanospheres [19], gold nanoparticles [20], MoS2 nanosheets [21] and CuO nanoparticles [22], have gained widespread application as efficient POD like nanozyme, due to several advantages, including simple preparation, robust stability, high catalytic activity, and excellent biocompatibility [23], which exhibits great potential in colorimetric analysis.

On the other hand, due to its ability to catalyze the oxidation of TMB and generate a plethora of free radicals, especially the most toxic hydroxyl radicals [24–26], POD-like nanozymes might induce toxic effects against bacteria [27,28], potentially addressing the issue of bacterial resistance. Currently, bacterial infections have emerged as fatal concerns for humanity, and existing strategies such as antibiotics [29], metal ions [30], and quaternary ammonium compounds [31] exhibit ecological toxicity, high costs, and environmental pollution [32]. Moreover, the escalating bacterial resistance poses a severe threat to public health. In contrast, nanozymes, as low-cost, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly materials, hold unique POD-like catalytic properties, enabling them to assist H2O2 in combating resistant bacteria in complex scenarios [33].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials due to their notable biocompatibility, wide precursor availability, low cytotoxicity, and ease of surface modification [34]. Doping CDs with metal ions introduces new active sites and electronic properties, significantly enhancing their catalytic activity and improving nanozyme performance [8]. Common methods for synthesizing CDs include hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal methods, and microwave-assisted synthesis [35]. Hydrothermal treatment is the most widely used techniques to fabricate CDs, due to its efficiency, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness [36]. However, limited research exists on directly doping metals into CDs to enhance their catalytic performance. Therefore, developing metal-doped CD nanozymes through one-step synthesis methods and exploring their multifunctional applications in nanobiotechnology is of great significance.

In this work, we synthesize Fe ions doped carbon dots (Cit-Fe-CDs) with excellent POD activity using iron-coordinating ferric ammonium citrate and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) via a hydrothermal method. Cholesterol can be catalyzed by cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) to generate H2O2 and in the presence of generated H2O2, Cit-Fe-CDs catalyze the oxidation reaction of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), allowing for colorimetric detection of cholesterol. In addition to cholesterol detection, Cit-Fe-CDs have been discovered to exhibit antibacterial activity against Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). The antimicrobial effect of Cit-Fe-CDs is attributed to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals, which can disrupt bacterial cell membranes, interfere with metabolic processes, or produce toxic components [27,28].

Cit-Fe-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 54 mg of oPD and 132.5 mg of ammonium ferric citrate were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The obtained solutions were heated at 120 °C for 6 hours in a hydrothermal reaction vessel. After natural cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed for 24 hours in a 1000 Da dialysis bag against deionized water. The resulting solution was freeze-dried to obtain Cit-Fe-CDs. The concentration of Cit-Fe-CDs was represented by the concentration of Fe3+ ions, and the content of Fe3+ ions in Cit-Fe-CDs (25 μg/mL) obtained by ICP-OES was 2.7 %.

The POD like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs (25 μg/mL) was examined using TMB (1 mM), oPD (1 mM), and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS, 1 mM) as chromogenic reagents and H2O2 (1 mM) as the substrate. The colorimetric reaction was conducted at 37 °C in a HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M) for 5 minutes, followed by measuring of the samples' absorption spectra.

Cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) at a concentration of 0.2 U/mL (in phosphate-buffered saline at pH 7.4) was incubated with varying concentrations of cholesterol (dissolved in anhydrous ethanol, ranging from 0 to 800 μM) in a 37 °C water bath for 30 minutes. Following incubation for certain period, 25 μg/mL of Cit-Fe-CDs, 1 mM of the chromogenic substrate TMB and 0.2 M of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) were added to the reaction mixture. The reaction was allowed to proceed at 37 °C in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M) for 10 minutes, and absorbance was subsequently measured to determine the reaction's progress.

A single colony of MRSA was inoculated into Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and incubated overnight at 37°C with agitation until reaching the logarithmic growth phase (OD600). Subsequently, the culture was appropriately diluted with fresh LB broth to achieve an OD600 of 0.1. Various concentrations of Cit-Fe-CDs nanozyme solution were prepared in sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 5.0, 0.01 M). These solutions were then combined with equimolar volumes of the MRSA culture in a 96-well microplate, covering a concentration range from 0 to 125 μg/mL. The microplate was incubated at 37 °C with continuous shaking, and at regular one-hour intervals, small aliquots were sampled, and their OD600 values were measured using a spectrophotometer, documenting the values over time. Meanwhile, a colony counting method was employed. Different concentrations of Cit-Fe-CDs (ranging from 0 to 125 μg/mL) were prepared in sterile PBS (pH 5.0, 0.01 M). Fresh MRSA colonies were cultivated, and using a sterile cotton swab, colonies with an area of 1 cm2 were picked up and introduced into a 10 mL volume of sterile physiological saline. After thorough mixing, the resulting suspension was adjusted to achieve a concentration of 1×108 CFU/mL, as determined by an OD600 of 0.257. Further dilution was carried out using PBS buffer (pH 5.0, 0.01 M) to reach a final concentration of 1×107 CFU/mL, which was utilized for subsequent co-cultivation experiments. Small volumes of the Cit-Fe-CDs solution were then aseptically spotted onto sterile nutrient agar plates, and known volumes of the diluted MRSA culture were evenly spread across these plates. After 24 hours of incubation at 37 °C, bacterial colonies were enumerated, and CFU/mL values were calculated for each plate.

The antimicrobial efficacy of Cit-Fe-CDs against MRSA was assessed by the fluorescent nucleic acid staining agents SYTO 9 and propidium iodide (PI). The MRSA bacteria were firstly introduced into a LB liquid medium at 37 °C and 180 rpm. When the OD600 value of the bacterial solution reached 0.5, 1 mL of the bacterial solution was extracted and collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 2 min. Afterward, the bacterial cells were rinsed three times with a 0.9 % sodium chloride solution to eliminate any remaining remnants of the growth medium. The bacteria, which were resuspended at a concentration of 108 CFU/mL, were exposed to Cit-Fe-CDs at a concentration of 125 μg/mL in the presence of H2O2 at a final concentration of 100 µM for 24 hours. The bacterial cells were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 2 minutes and washed twice with PBS. A solution containing a combination of SYTO 9 and PI dyes (5 µL) was introduced to the cells. The mixture was then left at room temperature for 20 minutes, after which it was washed twice with PBS to eliminate any remaining free fluorescent nucleic acid staining agents. The cells were further examined using Gen5 Imaging.

The cover slips were initially submerged in a solution of 75 % (v/v) ethanol overnight. They were then rinsed three times with PBS solution to eliminate any remaining substances on the surface. The cover slips were plated to a 48-well plate, filled with MRSA suspension (108 CFU/mL, 1 mL) for 6 hours. Afterwards, the slips were rinsed with PBS three times. A solution of Cit-Fe-CDs at a concentration of 125 μg/mL in the presence of H2O2 at a final concentration of 100 µM was continued cultivation with MRSA for another 24 hours. The slips were then rinsed with PBS three times to ensure they were free of Cit-Fe-CDs. The bacteria were initially preserved by immersing them in a 3 % glutaraldehyde solution for 5 hours. They were then subjected to a series of ethanol solutions with increasing concentrations of 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and ultimately 100 % (v/v) to remove any remaining water. Lastly, the bacteria were chemically dehydrated using tert-butanol for a period of 20 minutes. Subsequently, all the cover slips were dried for one day and then coated with a thin layer of gold. The samples were observed by a SEM under high-vacuum conditions at 10 kV.

The Cit-Fe-CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of oPD and ferric citrate to introduce Fe(Ⅲ) into the carbon dots (CDs) with high loading content of Fe ions. Due to the quite strong coordination capacity between ferric and carboxyl of citric acid, the ferric citrate is considered as the carbon source and oPD can be used as the surface functional molecules. During the synthetic process, the presence of oPD would facilitate for polymerization of citric acid with oPD and subsequently carbonization to generate the CDs. And the Fe ions are also preserved in the framework of the CDs. Then multiple characterization techniques are applied to confirm the structure of the Cit-Fe-CDs and the presence of Fe ions within the ultra-small carbon framework.

After successful obtaining the Cit-Fe-CDs, the morphology and size of Cit-Fe-CDs are analyzed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It is obvious that the Cit-Fe-CDs are extremely small nanoparticles, displaying a nearly spherical shape with a uniform distribution and no noticeable aggregation ( Fig. 1 A). The average size of the Cit-Fe-CDs is estimated to be 2.49 nm (Fig. 1 A, inset). However, lattice fringes of the nanoclusters are not observed, which suggests the amorphous carbon structure doped with Fe ions within the core of Cit-Fe-CDs. The crystallinity of Cit-Fe-CDs is assessed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern of Cit-Fe-CDs exhibits a broad peak at around 20.8° (Fig. 1B), suggesting an amorphous carbon structure within the core of the CDs, which is consistent with the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1 A, inset). This amorphous nature of Cit-Fe-CDs can be attributed to the compact arrangement of surface CC and CO bonds in Cit-Fe-CDs [37,38].

Subsequently, the surface functional chemistry is characterized by fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in the Fig. 1 C, the FTIR spectra of Cit-Fe-CDs displays a broad peak at 3416 cm⁻¹ associated with O-H stretching vibrations and characteristic peaks between 600 and 400 cm⁻¹, revealing the presence of Fe-N bond in the Cit-Fe-CDs. Additional peaks related to water soluble groups, such as CO (1638 cm-¹), CC (1618 cm-¹), C-N (1340 cm-¹), and C-O (1120 cm-¹) are observed, which can be ascribed to stretching vibrations of various oxygen and nitrogen related chemical bonds within aliphatic compounds. The full XPS spectra of Cit-Fe-CDs are presented in Figure S1A, featuring as four primary peaks at 285.0 eV, 348.0 eV, 532.0 eV, and 710.0 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2p, respectively. This analysis confirms the compositions within Cit-Fe-CDs are C, N, O, and Fe. The fitting of high-resolution for N1s indicates that nitrogen predominantly existed in pyrrole and pyridine forms (Fig. 1D), which serve as anchor points for individual Fe atoms and facilitate for coordination with metal cations [39,40]. A distinct Fe-O peak at 529.5 eV is observed in high resolution O1s spectra, indicating oxygen atom is involved in the metal coordination [41]. And the high resolution Fe2p suggested an oxidized state of the metal, likely Feδ⁺(δ∼2) ions existed in Cit-Fe-CDs (Fig. 1E and Figure S1A, inset).

Furthermore, the stability of the Cit-Fe-CDs are evaluated using Zeta potential measurements, which reflect the magnitude of repulsive forces between particles [42]. In deionized water, Cit-Fe-CDs exhibit a Zeta potential of −25.3 mV, indicating fine stability. This potential is significantly higher than that observed in an HAc-NaAc buffer solution at pH 4 (-10.5 mV), attributed to the protonation of basic nitrogen species, resulting in a positive shift in Zeta potential [43]. Notably, the Zeta potential of Cit-Fe-CDs increased with increasing concentration (Figure S1B).

Subsequently, the optical properties are characterized accordingly. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cit-Fe-CDs exhibits an absorption peak at 295 nm in the (Fig. 1 F), which can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO functional groups within Cit-Fe-CDs. Upon excitation with 420 nm laser, Cit-Fe-CDs emit visible orange-green fluorescence (FL) (Fig. 1 F, inset). Notably, when excited at a wavelength of 440 nm, Cit-Fe-CDs exhibit an emission peak at 560 nm, which is consistent with the fluorescent color of the Cit-Fe-CDs (Fig. 1 F, inset). And the excitation spectra of Cit-Fe-CDs is measured and its optimal excitation wavelength is measured to be 445 nm. The absolute quantum yield (QY) of Cit-Fe-CDs is determined to be 0.34 %, while the absolute QY of undoped CDs synthesized by citrate acid and oPD is estimated to be 14.28 %. The ultra-low QY of Cit-Fe-CDs may be related to the fluorescence quenching effect of the Fe3+. In conclusion, the successful synthesis of Cit-Fe-CDs is achieved through a hydrothermal process using ammonium ferric citrate and oPD as precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD like and OXD-like activities of Cit-Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the metal doping is a common strategy to fabricate nanomaterials with excellent POD activity [8]. Herein, the POD like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs is firstly confirmed through a TMB colorimetric reaction. The co-existence of Cit-Fe-CDs and H2O2 induce the oxidation of colorless TMB, which result in the formation of blue ox-TMB with typical absorption peak at 652 nm ( Fig. 2A), which can be also confirmed by the digital images of each solution (Fig. 2A, inset). Additionally, the POD like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs is also verified using two other chromogenic substrates, namely ABTS and oPD (Fig. 2B), typical absorption spectra of their related oxidation product appeared, which is consistent with their typical color (Fig. 2B, inset). Then several parameters related to the POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs have been optimized in this work. When the concentration of TMB increase from 0.125 to 1.5 mM, a positive relationship between absorbance at 652 nm and concentration of TMB is observed (Figure S2A). Meanwhile, with the H2O2 concentration varying from 0.125 to 2 mM, there is a noticeable increase in absorbance at 652 nm. And maximum absorbance value can be reached at 1 mM H2O2 concentration (Figure S2B). Moreover, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm increase with the concentration of Cit-Fe-CDs (Figure S2C), demonstrating the concentration dependent catalytic behavior of the Cit-Fe-CDs.

Due to several works reported the superior OXD like activity of the Fe based nanozyme. They are capable of catalyzing reactions similar to natural enzymes. The OXD like nanozyme catalyze the oxidation of the substrate (electron donor) to corresponding oxidized product in the presence of oxygen (electron acceptor) under ambient conditions, accompanied with the generation of H2O or H2O2 [44]. Herein, we further investigate the OXD like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs with TMB as the catalytic substrate. In the absence of H2O2, Cit-Fe-CDs can also catalyze the oxidation of the chromogenic substrate TMB to generate blue product. As the concentration of Cit-Fe-CDs increased, there is an obvious rise for the absorbance of TMB at 652 nm (Fig. 2C). Similarly, as the concentration of TMB increased from 0.25 mM to 2 mM, there is also a progressive increase for absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. 2D).

In order to clarify the mechanism of POD like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs nanozymes, the steady-state catalytic kinetic constants are determined by the Michaelis-Menten equations (pH = 4.0). The time-dependent absorbance at 652 nm is recorded accordingly. The initial reaction rate (ν0) of the catalytic process is determined based on recording the changes of absorbance which follows the Lamber Beer law (Eq. 1,
ε
652 nm
= 39,000
M
− 1
∙
cm
− 1
for ox-TMB). The initial reaction rates with respect to H2O2 or TMB concentrations are plotted ( Figs. 3A and 3D), and then are fitted by the Michaelis-Menten equations (Eq. 2; Figs. 3B and 3E). The Michaelis constant (K m) and maximum velocity (V max) are determined by a Lineweaver-Burk plot (Eq. 3; Figs. 3C and 3F). At present, [E] is represented by the concentration of the Fe3+ obtained from ICP-OES. And K cat represents the catalytic rate constant determined by Eq. 4.
(1)
A = ε lc
(2)

cat
=
V
max
[ E ]

In accordance with the aforementioned equation, the fitted K m and V max values for Cit-Fe-CDs nanozyme (Figs. 3C and 3F) are presented in Table S1. Specifically, K m reflects the binding affinity of the nanozyme for its substrates, shedding light on its catalytic efficiency [45]. Additionally, a comparison of the kinetic constants with that of several previous works is presented in Table S1. Compared with other catalytic nanoparticles, Cit-Fe-CDs demonstrate an exceptionally low K m value (0.0338 mM), indicative of a high affinity between Cit-Fe-CDs and TMB. Furthermore, the catalytic efficiency of Cit-Fe-CDs towards H2O2 and TMB (defined as K cat/K m) is 0.0023 mM−1s−1 and 0.084 mM−1s−1, respectively, surpassing that of most nanozymes.

Then ROS involved in the TMB oxidation by the Cit-Fe-CDs is further studied. Herein, various scavengers, such as ascorbic acid (AA) to eliminate total ROS, DMSO to scavenge •OH, NaN3 to clear singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide dismutase (SOD) to eliminate O2•−, are used to validate the types of ROS generated by Cit-Fe-CDs [46]. As shown in the Fig. 2E, the AA is found to completely inhibit the pronounced absorbance in the typical 652 nm, affirming the involvement of ROS. Furthermore, the influence of SOD and NaN3 on TMB oxidation is negligible, while DMSO completely suppress the oxidation of TMB. These results confirm that •OH is the primary ROS involved in TMB oxidation. To further confirm that the Cit-Fe-CDs can catalyze the generation of •OH from H2O2, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) is employed as a capture probe [47,48], and formation of •OH is detected through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). The observation of a typical four-line spectrum with relative intensities of 1:2:2:1 for DMPO/•OH confirmed the generation of •OH in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 2F). Based on above results, we can conclude that the •OH derived from the Fenton reaction catalyzed by Cit-Fe-CDs for H2O2 is main ROS involved in the POD activity.

Subsequently, we investigate the stability of Cit-Fe-CDs under various conditions, which is essential for biomedical applications such as the detection of cholesterol and antibacterial application. We have found that their catalytic activity remains relatively stable within the pH range of 4–6. The Cit-Fe-CDs exhibit the highest POD like activity at pH 4.0 and about 90 % of the POD activity remains at pH at 5 and 6, indicating the highly stable and superior POD activity of the Cit-Fe-CDs. And the POD activity gradually decreases as pH further increases to 12, eventually reaching near-zero levels at higher pH values (Fig. 2G). This trend differs slightly from natural HRP, where the optimal pH of HRP is pH 5 [49]. Similarly, highest oxidase-like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs is observed at pH 4, the oxidase activity decreases dramatically with the pH further increase and only 10 % oxidase activity is observed at pH 5. (Fig. 2H). Interestingly, after undergoing 24 hours of acid treatment, the Cit-Fe-CDs not only did not exhibit any reduction in enzymatic activity, but instead showed a noticeable enhancement in their activity (Figure S3A), which may be attributed to the more buried catalytic active sites are recovered after the acidic treatment. As shown in Figure S3B, after three months of normal storage, both POD activity and oxidase-like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs remain nearly constant, suggesting the excellent long-term stability of the Cit-Fe-CDs.

The influence of temperature on the POD and oxidase activity of Cit-Fe-CDs and HRP is presented in Fig. 2I. With increase of temperature, the oxidase-like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs gradually decrease and optimal temperature is observed at 20 °C. Remarkably, the POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs increase with rising temperature and maintain extremely high activity even at 70 °C. In contrast, the activity of HRP increase with rising temperature, reaching its peak at 40 °C, and then obviously decrease as the incubation temperature continue rising to 70 °C. Therefore, compared with the natural HRP, the POD activity of Cit-Fe-CDs exhibit excellent thermal stability, indicating their great practical application potential.

Due to the analysis performance is closely related to the various condition involved in the detection, the parameters during the detection are optimized accordingly. As the enzymatic activity of ChOx increase, the absorbance of the TMB catalyzed by Cit-Fe-CDs gradually increase ( Fig. 4 A). Notably, at ChOx concentrations of 0.2 U/mL and 0.4 U/mL, the absorbance levels are very close to each other and reach a plateau. However, as the ChOx concentration continue to increase beyond this point, the absorbance gradually decreases. Additionally, the absorbance increases with extended reaction times, reaching a plateau around 30 minutes, as evident in Figure S4A and S4B.

Based on these observations, an optimal ChOx concentration of 0.2 U/mL and a reaction time of 30 minutes are selected for cholesterol detection. Furthermore, the mechanism of cholesterol detection is investigated. Comparative experiments reveal that the catalysis of TMB occurred only when cholesterol, ChOx, and Cit-Fe-CDs are all present in the system (Fig. 4B). This finding suggests that H2O2 is generated through the reaction between cholesterol and ChOx, subsequently catalyzed by the POD like activity of Cit-Fe-CDs to induce oxidation of TMB.

As shown in Fig. 4 C, the relative absorbance of cholesterol increases gradually as the concentration of cholesterol increases from 0 to 800 μM. The changes of absorbance at 652 nm exhibit a linear relationship relative to the cholesterol concentration within 10 and 100 μM (Figs. 4D and 4E). A fitting of the absorbance values results in the linear equation: A = 0.01431 C + 0.4095 (R2 = 0.997), where A represents absorbance and C represents cholesterol concentration. The detection limit is determined to be 0.061 μM (3 N/S), indicating that when the cholesterol concentration reaches 0.061 μM, the color change in the colorimetric reaction can be observed with the naked eye. The comparison of linear range and limits of detection for analyzing concentration of cholesterol by Cit-Fe-CDs with previous work is listed in Table S2. Compared to other work, Cit-Fe-CDs offer a wider detection range and a lower detection limit, highlighting their superior performance in cholesterol detection [18,23,50–53].

Further investigations are conducted to assess the interference of various substances commonly existed in blood, such as uric acid, urea, homocysteine, glycine, MgCl2, glutamic acid, glucose, dopamine, lysine, histidine, aspartic acid, and glutathione, on cholesterol detection. As depicted in Fig. 4 F, the absorbance at 652 nm is significantly higher in the presence of cholesterol compared to the interference substances. This indicates that the method exhibits excellent interference resistance and high selectivity in cholesterol detection.

To validate the accuracy and feasibility of cholesterol detection in human serum, tests are conducted using human serum samples. Using the standard addition method, different concentrations of cholesterol are added to serum samples, and the cholesterol content is subsequently determined. As shown in Table 1, the recovery rates of the added samples range from 78.85 % to 105.97 %. The measured cholesterol concentrations closely match the actual added concentrations, demonstrating the practical applicability of this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity and ROS generation of Cit-Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several works reported that the superior activity of POD would endow the Cit-Fe-CDs with excellent ROS generation capacity to show better antibacterial activities [54,55]. Hence, the antibacterial abilities of Cit-Fe-CDs is investigated in the following studies. As shown in Fig. 5 A, in the absence of Cit-Fe-CDs, MRSA displays a distinct growth pattern. A lag phase is evident within the first 5 hours, followed by a rapid proliferation phase after 5 hours. This proliferation continues until approximately 13 hours, after which the growth rate gradually decelerates. In sharp contrast to this, varied degrees of inhibition in the proliferation of MRSA are observed when Cit-Fe-CDs are observed. The latency phase of MRSA gets longer as the concentration of Cit-Fe-CDs further increases. MRSA displays no signs of proliferation after 24 hours of incubation at a concentration of 125 μg/mL of Cit-Fe-CDs. In brief, these observations suggest that the possible existence of a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) for Cit-Fe-CDs against MRSA, which is the lowest concentration of an antimicrobial agent that significantly inhibits microbial growth [56]. In this case, the MIC is likely around 125 μg/mL or slightly lower, as growth is markedly delayed at this concentration.

Simultaneously, the colony forming method is utilized to further assess the antimicrobial effect of Cit-Fe-CDs against MRSA, the logarithmic cell density is statistically analyzed based on the number of colonies in different samples after incubation. As shown in Figs. 5B and 5D, during the concentrations of 25 μg/mL-100 μg/mL, the colonies in the Cit-Fe-CDs group are lower than the 6log10 CFU/mL in the PBS group. When the concentrations of Cit-Fe-CDs up to 125 μg/mL, there are no visible colonies of MRSA was observed, which is much lower than the 6log10 CFU/mL in the PBS group, at which point Cit-Fe-CDs exhibited 5 logarithmic values of inactivated bacterial concentrations (Fig. 5B, inactivation > 99.999 %). In the absence of Cit-Fe-CDs, MRSA exhibited robust growth, and with the introduction of Cit-Fe-CDs, MRSA growth is progressively inhibited. Moderate concentrations (50 μg/mL) display significant inhibition, while higher concentrations (100 μg/mL and 125 μg/mL) exhibit effective bactericidal activity (Figs. 5B and 5D). These findings underscore the concentration-dependent impact of Cit-Fe-CDs on bacterial strains. The delayed growth observed at higher Cit-Fe-CDs concentrations aligns with observations in other studies on concentration-dependent antibacterial effects of various nanomaterials [27,57].

In the Live/Dead bacterial viability assay, fluorescence microscopy is employed to distinguish between live and dead bacterial cells ( Fig. 6 A). The control group, treated without Cit-Fe-CDs, exhibits predominantly green fluorescence due to the staining of live cells with SYTO 9. In contrast, the group treated with Cit-Fe-CDs shows a distinct shift to red fluorescence, indicative of dead cells stained with propidium PI. The overlap images show a significant red signal, clearly demonstrating the bactericidal effect of Cit-Fe-CDs. Further analysis is conducted using SEM to observe the morphological changes in MRSA cells. The SEM images of the control group display MRSA cells with smooth, intact surfaces (Fig. 6B), indicative of healthy and structurally sound bacterial cells. In contrast, the SEM images of the Cit-Fe-CDs treated group shows extensive morphological damage, including surface roughening, deformation, and complete cellular disruption. The result agrees with the detection of the colony counting method.

The ability of Cit-Fe-CDs to induce significant cell death and morphological damage at relatively low concentrations suggest the potential application as antibacterial agents against these bacterial strains, which is consistent with existing literature on the antimicrobial properties of nanozymes [58]. The antibacterial activity of Cit-Fe-CDs may be attributed to the generation of elevated concentrations of ROS (Fig. 2F), which can interfere with normal cell signal transduction or induce oxidative stress, leading to cellular damage [27]. In the intracellular reactive oxygen species assay (Fig. 5 C), the control group exhibits weaker fluorescence, indicating lower levels of ROS in untreated MRSA cells. However, the group treated with Cit-Fe-CDs shows a significant increase in fluorescence intensity, suggesting a substantial elevation in intracellular ROS levels, further demonstrating that the enhancement of ROS production correlates with the antimicrobial activity of Cit-Fe-CDs. These findings are of significant importance for the potential use of Cit-Fe-CDs as antibacterial agents in biomedical applications.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized Cit-Fe-CDs with high POD activity for colorimetric cholesterol detection. The synthesis of Cit-Fe-CDs is straightforward, cost-effective, and results in stable nanoparticles. The detection method is simple and sensitive. The obtained cholesterol detection range is 10–100 μM, with a detection limit of 0.061 μM. Furthermore, this method can be applied to the detection of cholesterol in real serum samples, demonstrating accuracy and feasibility. In addition to their application in cholesterol detection, Cit-Fe-CDs nanozymes possess significant antibacterial properties, displaying bactericidal effects against MRSA at a low concentration of 125 μg/mL, thereby expanding the potential applications of Cit-Fe-CDs. Overall, it provides an excellent platform for colorimetric detection of cholesterol, offering a practical and sensitive approach for cholesterol analysis and potentially contributing to solutions for antimicrobial concerns.

Zhi Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Peng Zhou: Investigation. Cao Li: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Qingyuan Yang: Methodology, Investigation. Zi-Qiang Xu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Qian Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xiaoying Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Bingbing Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22073025, 21603067), the Talent Research Project of Qilu University of Technology (2023RCKY177) and Guangzhou Science and Technology Planning Project (2024A04J5025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134955.
